interest: The interest behind this claim is that of equity advocates and low-SES/rural families who want early selection abolished because it functions as a status-sorting rather than talent-sorting mechanism.
value: Equity (equal opportunity independent of family background) is in tension with excellence/selection-based efficiency.
fear: The driving fear is that children with potential from low-status families are being permanently locked out of higher tracks by their origin rather than their ability, wasting human potential and reproducing class hierarchy.
affected: middle-class families who benefit from early selection
assumption: It assumes that the SES-achievement gradient observed at the point of selection reflects social sorting rather than genuine ability differences that themselves correlate with background, and that removing the gate would redistribute opportunity rather than merely relocate the sorting.
empirical_uncertainty: The direction of the effect is strongly supported (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006; Jakubowski et al. 2016 on Poland), but whether abolition improves net equity is contested because the scenario explicitly notes exit/relocation to non-state schools is not established as harmless and peer-effect gains are contested with a 10-15 year lag.
